text: This video is related to a young lady with a rectovaginal fistula after a vaginal delivery. She had a third degree tear repaired intraoperatively. Unfortunately, she experienced wound dehiscence with fecal incontinence and vaginal discharge of feces ten days after the delivery. The defect was researched and a colostomy was performed. After this, a rectovaginal fistula was diagnosed. The anal sphincter mechanism was grossly intact. As part of the study of the hair symptoms, we performed a pelvic ultrasound, which showed a less of one quadrant defect on the external anal sphincter and two quadrant defects on the internal one. As you know, there is no single technique of repair appropriated for all rectovaginal fistulas. The key is the assessment of anal sphincter function. Following the American guidelines for the management of rectovaginal fistula, there are different available techniques.

The rectal advancement flap has direct access to the high pressure side, but if the flap fails there is a potential risk of larger fistula. The vaginal approach is easier and allows a rectocele repair if required at the same time. Talking about Transperineal Repair is essentially to a conversion to a fourth degree tear, which is then repaired. Mostly, it's used when there is an extensive injury to both sphincters. Related to the Marshall's flap, it's usually used only after failure of previous repairs, when the dissection of a scarred tissue plane is difficult. The technique consists in the mobilization of labial fat pad and performing a tunnel to a rectovaginal septum. Coming back to our case, the plan was a transparent nail approach using a posterior fourchette incision, a direct repair of rectal and vaginal wall defects, and anterior lobartoplasty to interpose healthy tissue and to give more consistency to the tissue repair.

The patient is positioned lithophilically for the procedure. The surgeon uses an Eisenheimer retractor to expose the anal canal, cleans the area with betadine and a swab introduced using Russian forceps. The swab, attached with a proline stitch, is left in place to prevent fecal soiling. The surgeon examines the fistula, confirming it is two centimeters in diameter and located above the sphincter, making it suitable for primary repair.

The surgeon infiltrates the area with local anesthetic containing adrenaline. A transverse incision is made at the posterior fourchette along the mucocutaneous junction of the vagina, facilitating early wound closure and reducing the risk of infection.

The surgeon opens the rectovaginal septum and begins dissecting the mucosal plane to address the fistula defect. The vaginal opening of the fistula is circumcised, starting dissection on one side and then the other, leaving the center for last. Despite the use of local anesthetic with adrenaline and diathermy, venous bleeding occurs but is controlled with diathermy or stitches. The vaginal mucosa is divided for better exposure and to overcome the distal edge of the fistula. Dissection continues, complicated by fibrosis from previous inflammation, with a risk of creating new fistulas. Medicine-bound scissors are used to maintain the plane, and traction and contra-traction are applied using alleys and suction. Digital contra-traction by the surgeon is crucial to maintain the dissection plane. Blunt dissection is performed above the fibrosis until two centimeters of healthy tissue is obtained above the distal fistula edge.

The surgeon identifies the fistula on the vaginal side and performs a primary repair using two ovicole interrupted stitches. The levator muscle is visible on the lateral side of the space between the vagina and rectum. An anterior Lebertoplasty is executed with full thickness, using two OPDS interrupted stitches, ensuring the rectal and vaginal walls are not held.

The surgeon examines the anal sphincter intraoperatively, noting it is complete and consistent, thus no repair is needed. A drain is placed in the rectovaginal space to prevent hematomas, intended to be removed within twenty-four hours. The vaginal mucosa is closed using a continuous locking suture with a taper cut needle, followed by closure of the mucocutaneous incision with III-O Vicryl. The posterior fourchette incision is used to close the perineal wound, reducing the risk of infection compared to anterior approaches.
Thank you very much for your attention
